_meta.title: Berkeley Homeless Response Team: Coordination, Oversight, and Outcome Tracking
_meta.author: City Auditor
_meta.extraction_method: manual — source PDF is image-based; text extracted by reading
_meta.extraction_note: Partial extract — full PDF read during session but key facts captured from memory; verbatim quotes not preserved

summary: The City Auditor reviewed Berkeley's Homeless Response Team and found significant operational gaps: the HRT coordination function was paused for a material period, no outcome tracking system exists to measure whether programs are working, and the city operates a standalone homeless services infrastructure that duplicates Alameda County services without a formal analysis of whether consolidation would be more effective or efficient.

coordination_pause.finding: HRT coordination was paused for a significant period during the audit window
coordination_pause.note: A core operational function of the team was simply not happening; the audit does not assess how long or what the impact was

no_outcome_tracking.finding: No outcome tracking system in place — the city cannot measure whether homeless programs are working
no_outcome_tracking.note: Berkeley spends $21.7M+ annually across 33+ programs with no mechanism to assess return on that spending

county_fragmentation.finding: Berkeley operates standalone homeless services infrastructure parallel to Alameda County services, with documented fragmentation and coverage gaps
county_fragmentation.note: No formal consolidation analysis has been conducted; the city maintains its own apparatus rather than leveraging county scale

attribution_note.finding: Findings are primarily staff-operational — the failures documented are in program management, not in specific council votes
attribution_note.note: Becomes individually scored when council members are asked about outcomes and claim ignorance, or when contracts are renewed without performance metrics being required

auditor_recommendations: Implement an outcome tracking system for homeless programs before renewing contracts
Commission a consolidation study comparing Berkeley's standalone infrastructure to Alameda County service delivery
Require performance benchmarks as a condition of program contract renewal
